title: The first thing I do when I get home
summary: Describe what you do right after arriving home and explain why you do it.
topic: The first thing I do when I get home
prompt: Write 8–10 sentences (150–250 words) about the first thing you do when you arrive home. Describe it in detail and explain why you do it. Use present simple for habits and 'because' or 'so' to give reasons.
hintStarters: The first thing I always do when I get home is…

hintVocab.meaningVi: một cách tự động, không cần suy nghĩ
tự ra hiệu cho bản thân

sampleText: The first thing I always do when I get home is change into comfortable clothes. I live in Seoul, and I work in an office, so every day I wear formal clothes for about ten hours. The moment I walk through the front door, I drop my bag by the sofa, go straight to the bedroom, and change into soft trousers and an old T-shirt. I do this almost on autopilot — I do not even think about it. It takes about two minutes. I think the reason I do it is because the clothes feel like a signal to myself. When I am wearing office clothes, I am still in work mode. When I change, something in my brain switches off. After I change, I usually go to the kitchen and turn on the kettle. Sometimes my flatmate is home and we talk while the water boils. Sometimes I am alone and I just stand at the window for a minute. Both are fine. But the changing comes first, always. Before I started doing this, I used to sit in my work clothes for hours and I never felt fully relaxed at home. Now the moment I change, I feel like I am somewhere different — even though it is the same apartment.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: thay sang quần áo thoải mái
quần áo công sở, lịch sự

sampleAnnotations.phrase: drop my bag by the sofa
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: đặt túi xuống cạnh ghế sofa
gần như một cách tự động
một tín hiệu cho bản thân tôi

sampleAnnotations.phrase: something in my brain switches off
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: điều gì đó trong não tôi tắt đi
việc thay quần áo luôn đến trước
cảm thấy thực sự thư giãn

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present simple for habits + 'because' for reasons
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng present simple để mô tả thói quen hàng ngày (I change, I drop, I go). Dùng 'because' để giải thích lý do (because the clothes feel like a signal). Dùng 'so' để nói về kết quả (I wear formal clothes, so I need to change).
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: Use present simple for daily habits ('I change', 'I drop', 'I go'). Use 'because' to give a reason ('because the clothes feel like a signal') and 'so' for a result ('I wear formal clothes, so I change when I get home').

sampleTranslationVi: Điều đầu tiên tôi luôn làm khi về nhà là thay sang quần áo thoải mái. Tôi sống ở Seoul và làm việc ở văn phòng, nên mỗi ngày tôi mặc quần áo công sở khoảng mười tiếng. Ngay khi bước qua cửa trước, tôi đặt túi xuống cạnh ghế sofa, đi thẳng vào phòng ngủ và thay sang quần mềm và áo phông cũ. Tôi làm điều này gần như một cách tự động — tôi thậm chí không nghĩ về nó. Chỉ mất khoảng hai phút. Tôi nghĩ lý do tôi làm vậy là vì quần áo giống như một tín hiệu cho bản thân. Khi mặc quần áo công sở, tôi vẫn trong chế độ làm việc. Khi thay ra, điều gì đó trong não tôi tắt đi. Sau khi thay đồ, tôi thường ra bếp và bật ấm đun nước. Đôi khi bạn cùng phòng tôi ở nhà và chúng tôi nói chuyện trong khi nước sôi. Đôi khi tôi ở một mình và chỉ đứng bên cửa sổ một phút. Cả hai đều ổn. Nhưng việc thay đồ luôn đến trước. Trước khi bắt đầu làm vậy, tôi thường ngồi trong quần áo công sở nhiều tiếng và không bao giờ cảm thấy thực sự thư giãn ở nhà. Bây giờ ngay khi thay đồ, tôi cảm thấy như đang ở đâu đó khác — dù vẫn là căn hộ cũ.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct present simple: 'The first thing I ___ when I get home is change clothes.'
options: always do
am always doing
always did
was always doing
mcQuestions.explanation: Present simple ('always do') describes a regular habit.
mcQuestions.hint: Habits and routines use present simple.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly means to put on different clothing?
options: change into comfortable clothes
change to comfortable clothes
change for comfortable clothes
change from comfortable clothes
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Change into' is the correct phrasal verb for putting on different clothes.
mcQuestions.hint: Which preposition follows 'change' when you put on new clothes?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the correct sentence using 'because' to give a reason.
options: I change clothes because the clothes feel like a signal.
I change clothes so the clothes feel like a signal.
I change clothes although the clothes feel like a signal.
I change clothes while the clothes feel like a signal.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Because' introduces the reason for an action.
mcQuestions.hint: Which linker introduces a reason or cause?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence describes doing something automatically without thinking?
options: I do this almost on autopilot.
I do this almost on automatic.
I do this almost on auto.
I do this almost on automation.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'On autopilot' is the idiom for doing something automatically without conscious thought.
mcQuestions.hint: Which is the natural English idiom for doing something automatically?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct verb: 'I ___ my bag by the sofa when I come in.'
options: drop
fall
lay
push
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Drop your bag' means to put it down quickly or casually.
mcQuestions.hint: Which verb means to put something down quickly or casually?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase means to stop thinking like you are at work?
options: something in my brain switches off
something in my brain shuts down
something in my brain turns off
something in my brain closes off
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Switches off' is the most natural metaphor for the brain stopping work mode.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrasal verb most naturally describes a mental state stopping?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'used to' for a past habit?
options: Before I started doing this, I used to sit in my work clothes for hours.
Before I started doing this, I use to sit in my work clothes for hours.
Before I started doing this, I was used to sit in my work clothes for hours.
Before I started doing this, I used to sitting in my work clothes for hours.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Used to' is followed by the base infinitive ('sit').
mcQuestions.hint: After 'used to', always use the base form of the verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word fills the gap? 'Now the moment I change, I feel ___ I am somewhere different.'
options: like
as
that
when
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Feel like + clause' is natural in informal English.
mcQuestions.hint: In informal English, which word follows 'feel' before a clause?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct way to describe the location of an object after putting it down.
options: I drop my bag by the sofa.
I drop my bag beside to the sofa.
I drop my bag near from the sofa.
I drop my bag close the sofa.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'By' means 'next to' or 'beside', and it does not need a preposition before the noun.
mcQuestions.hint: Which preposition means 'next to' without needing a second preposition?

options: I feel like I am somewhere different, even though it is the same apartment.
I feel like I am somewhere different, even though it is not the same apartment.
I feel like I am somewhere different, even so it is the same apartment.
I feel like I am somewhere different, although but it is the same apartment.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even though' introduces a contrast: feeling different despite being in the same place.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'even though' with the correct contrasting idea?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the best way to emphasize that one action always happens before others.
options: But the changing comes first, always.
But the changing is firstly, always.
But the changing come first, always.
But the changing coming first, always.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Comes first' is correct because 'the changing' is a gerund phrase (singular), and placing 'always' at the end adds emphasis.
mcQuestions.hint: Use the third-person singular form ('comes') — 'the changing' is a singular subject.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Do you have a ritual for separating 'work time' from 'home time'? Do you think this kind of boundary is important? What happens when people don't have one?
